how_it_works: VoiceAgentSession (voice-agent-session.ts:192) is the orchestrator: it constructs a PipelineBusImpl (pipeline-bus.ts:93) with three priority routes (Critical/Main/Background, Route enum at pipeline-bus.ts:23) and wires ~25 bus handlers in wireBusHandlers() (:454). User audio fans out to record/vad/stt (+eos when endpointingOwner!=provider_stt) at :524; STT/EOS finalize a turn via handleTurnComplete (:671), which (after a duplicate-EOS guard keyed on lastFinalizedContextId :672) pushes user.input to drive the LLM. LLM deltas are buffered into speakable sentences by takeCompleteVoiceText (voice-text.ts:18) in bufferTtsText (:776) and flushed on llm.done (:751). TtsPlayoutClock (tts-playout-clock.ts:13) tracks which contexts are still audible so TurnArbiter (turn-arbiter.ts:69) can gate barge-in; on commit it emits interrupt.detected, and handleInterruptDetected (:919) pushes stopIdleTimeout+truncate+interrupt.tts+interrupt.llm on Route.Critical (batched up to criticalBatchSize=4 per drain tick, dequeueBatch :276). The bus drain loop (start() :193) awaits each consumer handler serially per Main packet but re-checks Critical first every iteration, so interrupts preempt between Main packets. Watchdogs (voice-agent-session-util.ts:78) own STT-force-finalize, VAQI-missed-response, TTS-stall, and input-cadence timers.

findings.title: Per-turn dedup Sets grow unbounded for the session lifetime (only cleared on close)
findings.file_line: voice-agent-session.ts:210,224,226 (add sites :864,:920,:996; clear only at closeOnce :386-389)
findings.evidence: private interruptedGenerationContextIds = new Set<string>(); private firstTtsAudioFired = new Set<string>(); private fallbackInjectedContexts = new Set<string>(); ... handleTtsAudio adds: this.firstTtsAudioFired.add(pkt.contextId); handleInterruptDetected adds: this.interruptedGenerationContextIds.add(pkt.contextId); maybeSpeakErrorFallback adds: this.fallbackInjectedContexts.add(contextId). grep for '.delete(' shows NO per-context deletion for these three Sets — they are only emptied by closeOnce() at :386-389. firstLlmDeltaReceived and ttsTextBuffers ARE deleted per turn (:803,:801); these three are not.
findings.recommendation: These accumulate one interned contextId string per turn for the entire call. A long telephony session (hundreds/thousands of turns) leaks monotonically. Delete each context's entry at turn end: drop interruptedGenerationContextIds/firstTtsAudioFired/fallbackInjectedContexts in flushTtsText() (or on the next eos.turn_complete for that context), mirroring how firstLlmDeltaReceived is cleaned at :803. Keeping interruptedGenerationContextIds bounded also keeps the late-arriving-packet ignore-guard (:719,:752,:855) cheap.
findings.checklist_ref: ARCH (state hygiene / no unbounded growth); REL-09 backpressure spirit (PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md:330)

findings.title: Declared public events agent_first_audio and state_changed are never emitted
findings.evidence: VoiceAgentSessionEvents declares: agent_first_audio: (event: { tsMs: number; turnId: string }) => void; and state_changed: (event: { tsMs: number; from: SessionState; to: SessionState }) => void;  grep 'agent_first_audio|state_changed' over the file returns ONLY these two declaration lines — no this.emit('agent_first_audio',...) and no this.emit('state_changed',...) anywhere, even though handleTtsAudio at :863 detects first audio (!this.firstTtsAudioFired.has) and _state is assigned in 7 places (:346,:361,:365,:377,:402, etc.).
findings.recommendation: Either emit them where the state already exists (agent_first_audio inside the !firstTtsAudioFired branch at :863; state_changed at each this._state = assignment) or remove them from the public events type. Client consumers wiring on('agent_first_audio') / on('state_changed') per the contract receive nothing today — a silent integration breakage.
findings.checklist_ref: ARCH (event taxonomy completeness / public contract honesty)

findings.title: VAD speech_started clears latency-filler turn but speech may never sustain to a real barge-in
findings.evidence: handleVadSpeechStarted: if (this.latencyFiller.isFillerOnly(this.currentTurnId)) { this.cancelLatencyFillerTurn(this.currentTurnId, pkt.timestampMs); } and cancelLatencyFillerTurn unconditionally calls this.turnArbiter.emitInterruptDetected(contextId). This fires interrupt.detected on the very FIRST vad.speech_started frame, bypassing the minInterruptionMs sustained-speech gate that every other barge-in path honors (turn-arbiter onSpeechStarted :81 / onSpeechActivity :97). A transient noise blip during a filler-only turn commits a full interrupt.
findings.recommendation: Route filler cancellation through the same TurnArbiter gate rather than emitting interrupt.detected immediately, or only cancel the filler (not emit interrupt) until the arbiter confirms sustained speech. As-is, latencyFillerEnabled sessions can false-interrupt the filler on noise. Note: filler is off by default (latencyFillerEnabled===true required, :117/:243), which is why this is low.
findings.checklist_ref: ARCH barge-in gating; PRODUCTION-CHECKLIST.md:192 (cancel logic+media only on REAL interruption)

findings.title: Background-drop metric is itself enqueued onto the Background queue under overflow, can be dropped or compound pressure
findings.evidence: push() drops oldest on Background overflow (:144-151) then after q.push(p) calls enqueueBackgroundDropMetric(droppedForMetric). That method does its OWN bound check (:259 if (this.background.length >= this.bgCapacity) this.background.shift();) and pushes the metric. Under sustained Background saturation the very metric announcing the drop competes for the same full queue and shifts out another real packet — the drop signal can evict a second background packet or be evicted itself, so drop accounting is lossy exactly when it matters most.
findings.recommendation: Acceptable for a droppable lane, but consider surfacing background drops via the onBackgroundDrop callback / a dedicated counter rather than re-injecting a packet into the saturated queue, so the drop signal does not amplify the pressure it is reporting. Low because Background is droppable by design and onBgDrop already fires (:146).

summary: The core orchestration is genuinely solid in its hard parts: the three-route priority bus has correct strict drain order (Critical preempts between every Main packet via dequeueBatch :276; verified by pipeline-bus.test.ts:41), Critical-route interrupt fan-out (stop-idle/truncate/tts/llm) is correctly ordered and unbounded, handler errors are isolated and re-surfaced as pipeline.error rather than aborting the loop (:348), the TtsPlayoutClock correctly defers to real paced-playout over the sample-duration estimate (tts-playout-clock.ts:58) so the assistant stays interruptible until actually heard, the duplicate-EOS guard (:672) and the late-packet ignore guards after interrupt (:719/:752/:855) are sound, and voice-text sentence segmentation is pure/well-factored. The fallback-recursion guard (FALLBACK_CONTEXT_SUFFIX :994) and once-per-turn fallback (:995) are correct. The one real reliability hole is unbounded per-session growth of three dedup Sets (interruptedGenerationContextIds / firstTtsAudioFired / fallbackInjectedContexts) that, unlike their siblings, are never deleted per turn and only cleared at close() — a slow monotonic leak on long calls (HIGH). Plus two declared public events (agent_first_audio, state_changed) are never emitted, a silent client-contract gap (MEDIUM). Two LOW items: filler-cancellation bypasses the barge-in sustained-speech gate (off by default), and the background-drop metric re-enters the saturated queue it reports on. No critical/data-loss/talk-over-user defects found in this domain.
